Now I see the pattern! After clicking on the red block on the right panel AGAIN:
- Row 18-19: gggg (gray) - unchanged
- Row 20-23: bbbb (blue) + ggYY/gggg (gray container with yellow) - unchanged

- Row 34-37: bcb (blue/charcoal pattern) - unchanged
- Row 38-43: Gray container with green - unchanged
So clicking on the red block on the right panel TOGGLES the red block's position:
- First click: Red block moves UP from rows 30-33 to rows 24-29
- Second click: Red block moves DOWN from rows 24-29 back to rows 30-33
This is a toggle mechanism! Clicking on the red block on the right panel toggles the red block's position on the left panel.
Similarly, clicking on the blue block on the right panel toggles the blue block's position on the left panel:
- First click: Blue block and blue/charcoal pattern swap positions
- Second click: They swap back
Now I understand the mechanics:
- Clicking on right panel objects toggles the corresponding object's position on the left panel
- The green square is movable with arrow keys

sections.content:

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try the sequence: click red, then click blue
# to see if this arranges the blocks correctly

print("Step 1: Click red on right panel...")
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 48}])
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

# Check state
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nLeft panel after red click:")
for i in range(18, 45):
    print(f"  {i}: {ascii_lines[i][6:26]}")

print("\nStep 2: Click blue on right panel...")
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 48}])
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

# Check state
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nLeft panel after blue click:")
for i in range(18, 45):
    print(f"  {i}: {ascii_lines[i][6:26]}")

# Check if level completed
print(f"\nLevel completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
